preamble.summary: Psalm 3 is the first psalm attributed to David and the first individual lament in the Psalter. Its superscription places it during Absalom's rebellion, when David fled Jerusalem. Despite being surrounded by enemies who declare God will not save him, the psalmist confesses the LORD as his shield, lies down and sleeps in confidence, and calls on God to rise and deliver.
preamble.remarkable: This is the Psalter's first morning psalm — the speaker has slept through the night despite being surrounded by enemies and wakes to declare trust. The act of sleeping when threatened is itself an act of faith: the person who can sleep in danger has transferred responsibility to God. The structure moves from crisis (vv. 2-3) through confession of trust (vv. 4-5) to confidence (vv. 6-7) to petition and blessing (vv. 8-9). This movement from lament to trust becomes the template for dozens of psalms that follow.
preamble.friction: The superscription (verse 1 in Hebrew) attributes this psalm to David's flight from Absalom (2 Samuel 15-18). Whether this reflects historical memory or later editorial framing is debated. The superscriptions were likely added by editors who connected psalms to episodes in David's life. The theological content of the psalm works with or without the historical setting — it is the prayer of any person surrounded by enemies who declare that God has abandoned them.
preamble.connections: The Absalom narrative is in 2 Samuel 15-18. The phrase 'salvation belongs to the LORD' (v. 9) echoes Jonah 2:9. The 'shield' metaphor for God appears in Genesis 15:1 (God to Abraham: 'I am your shield'). The cry 'Rise up, O LORD' echoes Numbers 10:35, the ancient battle cry when the Ark advanced. Selah appears three times — its first occurrence in the Psalter.

verses.text_hebrew: מִזְמ֥וֹר לְדָוִ֑ד בְּ֝בׇרְח֗וֹ מִפְּנֵ֤י ׀ אַבְשָׁל֬וֹם בְּנֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: A Psalm of David, when he fled from Absalom his son.
verses.rendering:
A psalm of David,
when he fled from his son Absalom.
translator_notes: This is the first superscription in the Psalter that provides both authorship (le-David, 'of David' or 'for David' or 'belonging to David') and a historical occasion. The preposition le- is ambiguous: it could indicate authorship, dedication, or collection. The historical note connects the psalm to 2 Samuel 15-18, where David fled Jerusalem barefoot and weeping as his son seized the throne.

verses.text_hebrew: יְ֭הוָה מָה־רַבּ֣וּ צָרָ֑י רַ֝בִּ֗ים קָמִ֥ים עָלָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: LORD, how are they increased that trouble me! many are they that rise up against me.
verses.rendering:
O LORD, how many are my enemies!
Many are rising against me.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm plunges immediately into crisis. The exclamation mah rabbu tsarai ('how numerous are my adversaries!') is not a question but a cry of astonishment — the enemies have multiplied beyond expectation. The verb rabbu ('they are many, they have increased') is repeated in the parallel rabbim qamim alai ('many are those rising against me'). The verb qamim ('rising') is military language — troops rising for battle, enemies standing up to attack. David (or any besieged person praying this psalm) faces not a single opponent but a mass uprising. The prayer begins where real prayer often begins: with an honest description of how bad things are.
translator_notes: The address to YHWH at the opening establishes this as prayer, not mere complaint. The psalmist does not describe his situation to a human audience but brings it directly to God. This directness — naming the crisis before the sovereign — is the defining characteristic of biblical lament.

verses.text_hebrew: רַבִּ֗ים אֹמְרִ֣ים לְ֭נַפְשִׁי אֵ֤ין יְשׁוּעָ֓תָה לּ֬וֹ בֵאלֹהִ֬ים סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Many there be which say of my soul, There is no help for him in God. Selah.
verses.rendering:
Many are saying about me,
"There is no deliverance for him from God."
Selah.
verses.expanded_rendering: The enemies' attack is theological, not merely physical. They say le-nafshi ('about my soul, about my life, concerning me'): ein yeshu'atah lo b-Elohim ('there is no salvation for him in God'). This is the cruelest cut — not 'we will defeat you' but 'God will not save you.' The enemies have rendered a theological verdict: God has abandoned this person. For someone whose identity is anchored in divine relationship, this declaration strikes at the root. If God will not save, then nothing will. The word yeshu'ah ('salvation, deliverance, rescue') is the key term the entire psalm will answer — the enemies deny it; the psalmist will affirm it in verse 9. Selah appears here for the first time in the Psalter; its meaning remains uncertain, but it likely signals a musical or liturgical pause.
translator_notes: Selah (selah) appears 71 times in the Psalter and 3 times in Habakkuk 3. Its meaning is debated: it may derive from salal ('to lift up') indicating a musical crescendo, or from salah ('to pause') indicating a break for reflection. The Septuagint renders it diapsalma ('interlude'). We transliterate rather than translate because no consensus exists.

The phrase ein yeshu'atah lo b-Elohim is a direct denial of the most basic confession of Israelite faith: that God saves. In the Absalom context, this echoes the counsel of those who believed God had transferred favor from David to his son (2 Samuel 16:8, Shimei's curse).

key_terms.semantic_range: uncertain — possibly: lift up (voices or instruments), pause, interlude, forever
key_terms.note: selah's meaning has been debated for over two millennia. The Talmud suggests it means 'forever.' Modern scholars generally treat it as a liturgical or musical direction. Its placement often coincides with shifts in mood or topic within a psalm, suggesting it marks a pause for reflection or instrumental interlude.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאַתָּ֣ה יְ֭הוָה מָגֵ֣ן בַּעֲדִ֑י כְּ֝בוֹדִ֗י וּמֵרִ֥ים רֹאשִֽׁי׃
verses.text_kjv: But thou, O LORD, art a shield for me; my glory, and the lifter up of mine head.
verses.rendering:
But You, O LORD, are a shield around me,
my glory, the One who lifts my head.
verses.expanded_rendering: The turning word is ve-attah ('but You') — a sharp pivot from what the enemies say to what the psalmist knows. Against the many voices declaring 'no deliverance,' one voice confesses: YHWH magen ba'adi ('the LORD is a shield around me'). The magen ('shield') is not a small buckler but a full-body protective covering — ba'adi means 'around me, on my behalf.' Then two more titles: kevodi ('my glory') — the LORD is the source of the psalmist's honor, dignity, and weight in the world; and merim roshi ('the lifter of my head') — when shame and defeat press the head down, God lifts it back up. Three images in one verse: protection (shield), dignity (glory), and restoration (lifted head). Each one directly counters the enemies' claim that God has abandoned him.
translator_notes: The phrase kevodi ('my glory') is striking — the psalmist attributes his kavod ('glory, honor, weight, significance') entirely to God. If David is the speaker, this is a king acknowledging that his royal dignity derives not from the throne but from the LORD. The enemies may have stripped him of crown and city, but they cannot strip him of the glory that comes from God.

Merim roshi ('the lifter of my head') is a gesture of restoration. In the ancient world, a defeated person hung their head; to have one's head lifted was to be vindicated, acquitted, restored to honor (cf. Genesis 40:13, where Pharaoh 'lifts the head' of the cupbearer to restore him).

verses.text_hebrew: ק֭וֹלִי אֶל־יְהוָ֣ה אֶקְרָ֑א וַיַּֽעֲנֵ֨נִי מֵהַ֖ר קׇדְשׁ֣וֹ סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: I cried unto the LORD with my voice, and he heard me out of his holy hill. Selah.
verses.rendering:
With my voice I cry out to the LORD,
and He answers me from His holy mountain.
Selah.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist moves from confession to testimony. Qoli el YHWH eqra ('with my voice to the LORD I cry') — the emphasis on qoli ('my voice') is deliberate: in a world of many voices saying God will not help, the psalmist's own voice cries to God. And the result: vaiya'aneni me-har qodsho ('and He answers me from His holy mountain'). The verb ya'aneni ('He answers me') is in the imperfect tense, suggesting habitual or ongoing action — God answers, has answered, keeps answering. The answer comes from har qodsho ('His holy mountain') — Zion, the place of God's dwelling and the seat of His rule. Even though the psalmist may be fleeing Zion (if the Absalom setting holds), God's answer still comes from there. Distance from the holy mountain does not mean distance from God.
translator_notes: The tension between David fleeing from Jerusalem (the superscription's setting) and God answering from His holy mountain is theologically productive. The physical holy mountain may be in enemy hands, but God is not confined to His mountain. He answers from Zion even when the worshiper cannot reach Zion.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲנִ֥י שָׁכַ֗בְתִּי וָֽ֫אִישָׁ֥נָה הֱ֭קִיצוֹתִי כִּ֖י יְהוָ֣ה יִסְמְכֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: I laid me down and slept; I awaked; for the LORD sustained me.
verses.rendering:
I lay down and slept;
I woke again, for the LORD sustains me.
verses.expanded_rendering: This verse is the psalm's theological center, and its power lies in its simplicity. Three verbs trace a single night: shakhavti ('I lay down'), va-ishanah ('and I slept'), heqitsoti ('I woke up'). In the context of being surrounded by enemies who declare God has abandoned him, the act of sleeping is an extraordinary statement of trust. Sleep requires surrender of vigilance — the person who sleeps in danger has decided that God is keeping watch. The reason is stated plainly: ki YHWH yismekeni ('for the LORD sustains me, supports me, upholds me'). The verb samakh means to lean on, to support, to prop up. The LORD is the structural support that allows the psalmist to close his eyes. This is not theological theory but embodied trust — the kind that shows in the body's willingness to become unconscious when threat is real.
translator_notes: The three verbs (lay down, slept, woke) echo the daily rhythm of Deuteronomy 6:7 ('when you lie down and when you rise up'). The psalmist's faith is not a crisis-mode exception but woven into ordinary physical experience. Psalm 4:9 will make the same connection between trust and sleep.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹ֣א אִ֭ירָא מֵרִבְב֥וֹת עָ֑ם אֲשֶׁ֥ר סָ֝בִ֗יב שָׁ֣תוּ עָלָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: I will not be afraid of ten thousands of people, that have set themselves against me round about.
verses.rendering:
I will not fear the tens of thousands of people
who have positioned themselves against me on every side.
verses.expanded_rendering: The confidence built through sleep now produces a declaration: lo ira ('I will not fear'). The numbers are staggering: rivevot am ('myriads of people, tens of thousands') — this is not a minor threat but a mass uprising. The verb shatu ('they have set, they have positioned') and the word saviv ('all around, on every side') describe complete encirclement. The psalmist is surrounded and vastly outnumbered, yet fear has been displaced by the trust established in the previous verse. The logic is not 'the enemy is weak' but 'God is strong.' The fearlessness is not bravado — it is the fruit of having slept under God's protection and awakened alive.
translator_notes: The progression from verse 2 ('how many are my enemies') to verse 7 ('I will not fear tens of thousands') shows the psalm's emotional arc. The same reality — massive opposition — is perceived differently after the confession of trust in verses 4-6. The circumstances have not changed; the psalmist's posture has.

verses.text_hebrew: קוּמָ֤ה יְהוָ֗ה הוֹשִׁ֥יעֵנִי אֱלֹהַ֑י כִּֽי־הִכִּ֤יתָ אֶת־כׇּל־אֹיְבַ֬י לֶ֗חִי שִׁנֵּ֖י רְשָׁעִ֣ים שִׁבַּֽרְתָּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Arise, O LORD; save me, O my God: for thou hast smitten all mine enemies upon the cheek bone; thou hast broken the teeth of the ungodly.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's only direct petition arrives: qumah YHWH ('rise up, O LORD') — an ancient battle cry echoing Numbers 10:35, when Israel called on God to scatter enemies as the Ark moved forward. Hoshi'eni ('save me, deliver me') is the verb form of the yeshu'ah that the enemies denied in verse 3. The psalmist asks for precisely what the enemies said was impossible. The motivation clause uses perfect tense verbs — hikkita ('You have struck') and shibarta ('You have shattered') — describing God's past actions as the basis for confidence in present deliverance. The imagery is visceral: striking enemies on the jaw (lechi) and shattering their teeth (shinne resha'im). Teeth are instruments of devouring; enemies who consume the innocent have their weapons broken. The violence is metaphorical but real — it describes the complete disabling of hostile power.
translator_notes: The cry qumah YHWH ('arise, O LORD') appears in Numbers 10:35, Psalm 7:7, Psalm 9:20, Psalm 10:12, and elsewhere. It is one of the oldest prayers in the Hebrew Bible, rooted in the tradition of the Ark of the Covenant leading Israel into battle. To ask God to 'arise' is to ask Him to act, to intervene, to make His power visible.

The perfect tense verbs ('You have struck,' 'You have shattered') may describe either past experience or prophetic confidence — the psalmist is so certain of God's action that he speaks of it as already accomplished. This 'prophetic perfect' is common in psalms of trust.

verses.text_kjv: Salvation belongeth unto the LORD: thy blessing is upon thy people. Selah.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm concludes with a confession that answers the enemies' denial in verse 3. They said 'there is no yeshu'ah for him in God'; the psalmist now declares la-YHWH ha-yeshu'ah ('to the LORD belongs salvation, deliverance is the LORD's to give'). The definite article on ha-yeshu'ah ('the salvation') makes it absolute: all genuine rescue comes from and belongs to God. The final line extends the blessing beyond the individual: al ammekha virkhatekha ('upon Your people is Your blessing'). The singular sufferer's prayer ends with the community's welfare — a movement from 'save me' to 'bless Your people' that reflects the Psalter's integration of individual and communal piety. Selah closes the psalm, marking a final pause.
translator_notes: The phrase la-YHWH ha-yeshu'ah echoes Jonah 2:10 (Hebrew v. 10), where Jonah declares from the belly of the fish: yeshu'atah la-YHWH ('salvation belongs to the LORD'). Both speakers make this declaration from a place of extremity — surrounded by enemies (David) or swallowed by the sea (Jonah). The confession that salvation is God's exclusive property is precisely the confession that desperation produces.

The shift from individual petition ('save me') to communal blessing ('upon Your people') is characteristic of Israelite prayer. The individual never prays in isolation from the community. Even in personal crisis, the psalmist remembers that he is part of a people.
